target.title: fix(commands): warn when doctor memorySearch.extraPaths point to missing paths
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds a `doctor` warning for memory search configurations that point at missing `extraPaths`.

Before this change, stale `extraPaths` could silently make file-backed continuity look broken: there was nothing to index or watch, but `openclaw doctor` did not surface the misconfiguration.

With this change, `doctor` now:

- resolves the agent workspace directory
- checks configured memory search `extraPaths`
- resolves relative paths against the workspace
- warns if any configured paths do not exist

The warning explains the impact and suggests two fixes:

- recreate the missing file/directory
- remove the stale path from `agents.defaults.memorySearch.extraPaths`

## Files changed

- `src/commands/doctor-memory-search.ts`
- `src/commands/doctor-memory-search.test.ts`

## Validation

Ran targeted tests:

```powershell
corepack pnpm vitest run src/commands/doctor-memory-search.test.ts

target.title: fix(feishu): prefer user_id for sender name lookup
target.body:
Summary
Feishu inbound events can carry both sender_id.user_id and sender_id.open_id.

OpenClaw currently resolves sender display names by looking up only the open_id path via contact.user.get(... user_id_type=open_id). In some tenant/group contexts, this can surface anonymized placeholder names such as 用户377052 even when a real human-readable name is available through the sender's user_id.

This patch changes sender-name resolution to:
Prefer sender_id.user_id when present
Fall back to sender_id.open_id
Fall back to the historical senderId path as a last resort
This preserves existing behavior for events that only carry one identifier, while improving sender attribution when both IDs are available.
Why
In real Feishu group usage, sender names were occasionally resolved to placeholder labels like 用户377052, which then leaked into the assistant-visible conversation context and downstream replies.

A likely cause is that name resolution always preferred open_id, even when user_id was available in the inbound event. In these cases, user_id is a better first choice for tenant-native sender lookup.
Changes
Add senderUserId to FeishuMessageContext
Update Feishu sender-name resolution to try lookup candidates in this order:
user_id
open_id
fallback senderId
Keep existing caching behavior
Add tests covering:
preferring user_id over open_id
falling back to open_id when user_id returns no usable name
Validation
Ran:
pnpm -C openclaw-upstream exec vitest run --config vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/feishu/src/bot.test.ts

Result:
57 passed

target.title: Cap global cache maps to prevent memory leaks in long-running processes
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Several global `Map` caches in the agent runtime grow indefinitely — they never evict entries. In long-running gateway/daemon processes, this is a slow memory leak.
- Why it matters: The gateway can run for days. Caches like `workspaceFileCache` store full file contents (up to 2MB each) and never clean up, eventually pressuring the heap.
- What changed: Added max-size caps with oldest-first eviction to four caches:
  - `bootstrap-cache.ts`: max 64 entries
  - `workspace.ts` (`workspaceFileCache`): max 128 entries
  - `cache-trace.ts` (`writers`): max 32 entries
  - `context.ts` (`MODEL_CACHE`): max 256 entries
- What did NOT change: Cache semantics are identical. Eviction only kicks in at counts well above normal operation. No TTL or LRU tracking — just a simple size cap.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Memory / storage
- [x] Gateway / orchestration

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None — caps are passive and only trigger at abnormally high entry counts.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Steps

1. `pnpm build` — clean
2. `pnpm test` — 17105 passed, all failures pre-existing
3. `pnpm format` — clean

### Expected

Build and tests unaffected.

### Actual

Confirmed.

## Evidence

- [x] `pnpm build` clean
- [x] `pnpm test` same pass/fail as main

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified: build, test suite, format
- Edge cases: cap values chosen to be well above typical counts (a workspace has ~9 bootstrap files, not 64; model registry has ~30 models, not 256)
- Not verified: long-running daemon to observe eviction in production

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- Revert this PR — no config to restore
- Symptom to watch: cache misses causing extra disk reads after eviction (performance, not correctness)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Evicting a still-needed cache entry causes a redundant disk read.
  - Mitigation: Caps are 5-10x above normal counts. The evicted entry will just be re-read on next access.

target.title: Harden tool schemas with maxItems and maximum bounds
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Many tool schema params lack upper bounds — arrays have no `maxItems`, numeric fields have no `maximum`. When the model hallucinates extreme values (thousands of array items, multi-hour timeouts), the gateway blindly forwards them.
- Why it matters: Unbounded params are a DoS vector and waste tokens on schema-level values that will never be valid.
- What changed: Added `maxItems` to all unbounded `Type.Array()` fields and `maximum` to numeric fields across `web-search.ts`, `browser-tool.schema.ts`, `message-tool.ts`, and `nodes-tool.ts`. Bounds are derived from API limits (e.g. Discord caps selects at 25 options, modals at 5 fields) or existing runtime constants.
- What did NOT change: No runtime validation or execution logic. Schema-only.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [x] Security hardening

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Skills / tool execution

## Linked Issue/PR

- Continues #62

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

None — these are schema hints for model tool-calling, not user-facing config.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Steps

1. `pnpm build` — clean
2. `pnpm test` — 17105 passed, all failures pre-existing
3. `pnpm format` — clean

### Expected

Build and tests unaffected.

### Actual

Confirmed.

## Evidence

- [x] `pnpm build` clean
- [x] `pnpm test` same pass/fail as main

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified: build, test suite, format
- Edge cases: bounds match Discord API limits (25 for selects/buttons, 5 for modal fields, 4000 for text length) and existing runtime constants (MAX_PHOTOS_LIMIT=20)
- Not verified: live gateway with real model calls hitting new bounds

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- Revert this PR — no config to restore
- Symptom to watch: tool calls rejected by schema validation that previously passed (unlikely, bounds are generous)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A bound is too tight for a legitimate use case.
  - Mitigation: All bounds are well above typical usage. Can bump per-field without side effects.

target.title: fix(auth): fix GitHub device flow polling and add --wait flag for WSL…
target.body:
…/remote terminals

The device flow polling loop had two bugs:

1. When GitHub returns slow_down, the response includes the new minimum polling interval in seconds. The old code ignored this and only added a fixed 2000ms to the original interval, causing continued rate limiting. Now intervalMs is updated to the server-specified value (seconds * 1000).

2. The loop polled immediately on the first iteration before the user had a chance to authorize, triggering authorization_pending which in turn caused slow_down cascades. Moving the sleep to the top of the loop fixes this.

Adds a --wait flag to login-github-copilot that prompts the user to confirm they've authorized before polling begins. Also prints the URL and code as plain text so they're easy to copy in terminals (e.g. WSL, SSH) where the clack box rendering prevents ctrl+click link detection.

Usage:
  openclaw models auth login-github-copilot --wait

 Summary

   - Problem: GitHub device flow polling fired immediately on the first iteration, causing an authorization_pending
  response that triggered GitHub's slow_down backoff, making logins unnecessarily slow.
   - Why it matters: WSL/remote terminal users can't auto-open a browser, so they need extra time to manually navigate
   to the URL — and the backoff made the flow even more painful.
   - What changed: Poll delay moved to the top of the loop (always wait first); slow_down now respects GitHub's
  returned interval value; new --wait flag prints plain-text URL+code and waits for user confirmation before polling
  begins.
   - What did NOT change: No changes to token storage, auth profile logic, or any non-GitHub-Copilot auth paths.

  Change Type (select all)

   - [X]  Bug fix
   - [X]  Feature

  Scope (select all touched areas)

   - [X]  Auth / tokens
   - [X]  UI / DX

  Linked Issue/PR

   - Closes #
   - Related #

  User-visible / Behavior Changes

   - openclaw models login --github-copilot now waits one polling interval before the first token request (previously
  polled immediately).
   - New --wait flag: prints verification URL and code as plain text, then prompts for confirmation before polling.
  Intended for WSL/remote terminals where the browser doesn't auto-open.
   - slow_down responses now use GitHub's returned interval value rather than adding a fixed 2000ms offset.

  Security Impact (required)

   - New permissions/capabilities? No
   - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
   - New/changed network calls? No (same endpoints, adjusted timing only)
   - Command/tool execution surface changed? No
   - Data access scope changed? No

  Repro + Verification

  Environment

   - OS: Linux (WSL2 / Ubuntu)
   - Runtime/container: Node.js
   - Model/provider: GitHub Copilot
   - Integration/channel: CLI device flow login
   - Relevant config: N/A

  Steps

   1. Run openclaw models login --github-copilot --wait
   2. Copy the printed URL and code, authorize in browser
   3. Confirm prompt in terminal

  Expected

   - Login completes without slow_down backoff errors; polling begins only after the initial interval has elapsed.

  Actual

   - Previously: immediate first poll → authorization_pending → slow_down backoff triggered.

  Evidence

   - [ ]  Failing test/log before + passing after
   - [ ]  Trace/log snippets
   - [ ]  Screenshot/recording
   - [ ]  Perf numbers (if relevant)

  Human Verification (required)

   - Verified scenarios: Device flow login with --wait flag on WSL2; confirmed URL/code printed as plain text;
  confirmed polling does not begin until user confirms.
   - Edge cases checked: slow_down response with and without interval field in body; expired token during polling.
   - What you did not verify: Non-WSL native terminal auto-open behavior; Windows native terminal.

  Review Conversations

   - [ ]  I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
   - [ ]  I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

  Compatibility / Migration

   - Backward compatible? Yes
   - Config/env changes? No
   - Migration needed? No

  Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

   - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert src/providers/github-copilot-auth.ts and src/cli/models-cli.ts
  to prior state.
   - Files/config to restore: src/providers/github-copilot-auth.ts, src/cli/models-cli.ts
   - Known bad symptoms: Login hangs indefinitely or never completes polling.

  Risks and Mitigations

   - Risk: Adding an initial delay before the first poll could slow down logins on fast connections where the user
  authorizes immediately.
    - Mitigation: The delay equals the server-specified interval (minimum 1s), which is GitHub's own recommended
  polling cadence — no change to the happy path timing in practice.

target.title: fix(agents): post full target reply to thread in sessions_send A2A flow
target.body:
When `sessions_send` targets a session with an announce target (e.g. Cursor Discord thread), post the **full target reply** to that channel first so the thread shows the actual reply instead of only the announce step's short ack (e.g. "Acknowledged."). Skip posting the announce reply when the full reply was already posted to avoid duplicate messages.

target.title: fix: pass through explicit OpenAI cache retention
target.body:
## Summary

Support explicit `cacheRetention` passthrough for **OpenAI** and **OpenAI-Codex** models.

Today, OpenClaw forwards cache retention for direct Anthropic and Anthropic-on-Bedrock, but drops it for OpenAI providers before the value reaches pi-ai. That prevents pi-ai from applying its existing OpenAI Responses mapping to `prompt_cache_retention: "24h"` when `cacheRetention: "long"` is configured.

This PR fixes that by making cache retention resolution provider-aware rather than Anthropic-only.

## What changed

- extracted cache retention resolution into a new provider-neutral helper:
  - `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/cache-retention.ts`
- updated `extra-params.ts` to import from the new helper
- kept a re-export from `anthropic-stream-wrappers.ts` for compatibility
- added focused tests for:
  - Anthropic default + explicit behavior
  - legacy `cacheControlTtl` mapping
  - Bedrock explicit-only behavior
  - OpenAI explicit passthrough
  - OpenAI-Codex explicit passthrough
  - unsupported providers remaining unchanged

## Behavior

### Anthropic
- unchanged
- still defaults to `"short"`
- still honors explicit `cacheRetention`
- still supports legacy `cacheControlTtl`

### Amazon Bedrock
- unchanged
- still explicit-only
- no implicit default added

### OpenAI / OpenAI-Codex
- new behavior: explicitly configured `cacheRetention` is now forwarded
- no implicit default added
- API-specific translation remains downstream in pi-ai

### Other providers
- unchanged
- `cacheRetention` remains ignored

## Motivation / credit

Inspired by the debugging and analysis in #27515.

Thanks to @sprfrkr for surfacing the OpenAI passthrough gap and tracing the path down to pi-ai's existing prompt-cache retention support. This PR implements the fix as a broader provider-aware resolver instead of a narrow provider-specific exception.

## Tests

Ran:

- `pnpm vitest run src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/cache-retention.test.ts`
- `pnpm vitest run src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/cache-retention.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/extra-params.cache-retention-default.test.ts`
target.author: jmcook

target.title: feat(plugins): add agent_to_agent_turn hook for A2A ping-pong visibility
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** During agent-to-agent (A2A) ping-pong exchanges, intermediate turns are invisible to channel plugins — only the final "announce" step goes through the channel. Channel plugins (e.g., DingTalk, Discord) cannot forward intermediate expert-to-expert discussion to users in real-time.
- **Why it matters:** For multi-expert collaboration scenarios (e.g., troubleshooting in a group chat), users need to see the discussion process between experts, not just the final conclusion. Without visibility into intermediate turns, the A2A feature cannot be used for user-facing expert collaboration.
- **What changed:** Added a new `agent_to_agent_turn` plugin hook that fires after each completed turn of an A2A ping-pong exchange. Channel plugins can register handlers via `api.on("agent_to_agent_turn", handler)` to intercept and forward these messages.
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to A2A core logic, session management, or existing hook behavior. The hook is purely additive and opt-in.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related: Multi-expert collaboration in channel plugins (DingTalk @sub-agent feature)

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- New plugin hook `agent_to_agent_turn` available for channel plugins
- No behavior change unless a plugin explicitly registers a handler
- Zero overhead when no handlers are registered (guarded by `hasHooks` check)

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No` — hook is read-only, receives reply text that already exists in the session
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS Darwin 24.6.0
- Runtime/container: Node.js
- Model/provider: N/A (infrastructure change)
- Integration/channel: DingTalk (primary use case)

### Steps

1. Configure two agents with `tools.agentToAgent.enabled: true` and mutual `allow` lists
2. Register a plugin handler: `api.on("agent_to_agent_turn", (event) => console.log(event))`
3. Trigger an A2A exchange via `sessions_send` with `timeoutSeconds > 0`

### Expected

- Handler is called after each ping-pong turn with `{ turn, speakerSessionKey, reply, ... }`
- A2A exchange completes normally regardless of handler success/failure

### Actual

- Verified via `pnpm build` — TypeScript compilation passes including exhaustiveness checks

## Evidence

- [x] `pnpm build` passes — TypeScript exhaustiveness check (`MissingPluginHookNames extends never`) confirms all type registrations are consistent
- [x] Hook follows exact same pattern as `subagent_spawned`/`subagent_ended` (void hook, `runVoidHook`, `hasHooks` guard)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `pnpm build` compilation passes, exhaustiveness type check passes
- Edge cases checked: hook handler errors are caught silently (cannot interrupt A2A exchange), `hasHooks` guard prevents overhead when no handlers registered
- What I did **not** verify: End-to-end A2A exchange with a real channel plugin handler (requires running OpenClaw instance with DingTalk plugin)

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes` — purely additive, no existing behavior changes
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the 3-file commit; or simply don't register the hook in plugins
- Files/config to restore: `src/plugins/types.ts`, `src/plugins/hooks.ts`, `src/agents/tools/sessions-send-tool.a2a.ts`
- Known bad symptoms: If the hook runner import path is wrong, build will fail (caught by CI)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Hook handler throws an error during A2A exchange
  - Mitigation: All hook calls are wrapped in `try/catch` — errors are silently swallowed, A2A exchange continues uninterrupted

---

> **Disclosure:** This PR was developed with AI assistance (Claude). All code has been reviewed and verified by @wjueyao.

target.title: fix(reply): use isSilentTokenOnOwnLine in all delivery paths
target.body:
## Problem

PR #42873 introduced `isSilentTokenOnOwnLine()` and applied it to `normalizeReplyPayload`, but several other code paths still use exact-match checks (`isSilentReplyText` or `.toUpperCase() === SILENT_REPLY_TOKEN`) that miss the `"text\n\nNO_REPLY"` pattern. This causes the preceding text to leak to the user as a visible message.

Confirmed in production: after applying #42873 locally (which fixes `normalizeReplyPayload`), the bug persisted through at least three other paths — the deliver layer, cron delivery dispatch, and gateway chat event handlers.

```
Model output:    "15 min since last check, nothing new.\n\nNO_REPLY"
User sees:       "15 min since last check, nothing new.\n\nNO_REPLY"
Expected:        (nothing — the model intended silence)
```

## Root cause

`isSilentTokenOnOwnLine()` was only applied in one place. Six other files gate NO_REPLY suppression with exact-match checks that don't recognize the own-line pattern.

## Fix

Import and use `isSilentTokenOnOwnLine()` alongside existing checks in all remaining paths:

| File | Function | What it gates |
|------|----------|---------------|
| `src/auto-reply/reply/reply-directives.ts` | `parseReplyDirectives` | Final delivery layer — last gate before messages reach the channel |
| `src/cron/isolated-agent/delivery-dispatch.ts` | Synthesized text checks | Cron job output suppression (interim + final) |
| `src/cron/isolated-agent/subagent-followup.ts` | Descendant reply collection + grace period | Cron sub-agent reply filtering |
| `src/gateway/server-chat.ts` | `emitChatDelta`, `flushBufferedChatDelta`, `emitChatFinal` | Streaming text suppression |
| `src/gateway/server-methods/chat.ts` | Chat history sanitization | Assistant message filtering |

## Changes

- 6 source files updated (+67/-13)
- 1 new test file: `reply-directives.test.ts` with 6 tests covering exact, own-line, inline, and normal text patterns
- All changes are additive: existing exact-match checks are preserved, own-line check is added with `||`

## Testing

- Locally tested via compiled dist patches on production gateway for 3 days
- Confirmed the deliver layer (`parseReplyDirectives`) was the final leak path after applying #42873
- New unit tests cover the key scenarios

## Relationship to #42873

This PR depends on `isSilentTokenOnOwnLine()` introduced in #42873. It extends the fix to the remaining 6 code paths. Together they provide complete coverage.

**AI-assisted** (Claude Opus 4.6) — fully tested, confirmed understanding of all changes.

Refs #42873, #42472, #30916, #33544

target.title: fix: skip Gemini CLI audio autodetect in headless flows
target.body:
## Summary
- stop auto-selecting Gemini CLI for audio transcription in headless media-understanding flows
- keep Gemini CLI auto-detect for image/video only
- add regression coverage proving audio falls through to provider transcription even when a `gemini` binary is present

## Why
Fixes #46588.

Gemini CLI headless audio-path prompting is currently unreliable for Telegram voice notes / `.ogg` inputs: it may treat the path as plain file context instead of binary audio and drift into invalid tool fallback behavior (`run_shell_command`). Until the CLI offers a deterministic headless binary-audio path, auto-detect should prefer local/provider audio transcription backends instead of Gemini CLI for audio.

## Testing
- `corepack pnpm vitest run src/media-understanding/apply.test.ts src/media-understanding/runner.auto-audio.test.ts`

target.title: feat(cron): cron job management improvements — edit, run history, pipeline orchestration (#6808)
target.body:
Closes #6808

## What changed

### 1. Edit existing cron jobs (bug fixes)
- Fixed hardcoded English strings in `validateCronForm` — failure alert
  threshold and cooldown errors now use i18n keys so they render
  correctly in all locales.
- Fixed missing `/* @vitest-environment jsdom */` pragma on `cron.test.ts`
  that caused `ReferenceError: document is not defined` when run under
  the root Vitest config.
- Fixed `Run if due` button text — was hardcoded English, now uses
  `t("cron.jobList.runIfDue")`.

### 2. View execution history (i18n + test registration)
- Added three cron test files to the root `vitest.config.ts` include
  list so they actually run in CI:
  `ui/src/ui/views/cron.test.ts`,
  `ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.test.ts`,
  `ui/src/ui/controllers/cron-filters.test.ts`
- Added i18n keys for `runIfDue`, `failureAlertAfterInvalid`, and
  `failureAlertCooldownInvalid` in en / es / zh-CN.
- Added regression test confirming error keys are i18n strings (not raw English).

### 3. Pipeline orchestration — job chaining ✨
Chain cron jobs together: when a job finishes it can automatically
trigger another job by ID, based on whether it succeeded or failed.

**Backend (`src/`)**
- `CronJobBase` gains `onSuccessJobId?: string` and `onFailureJobId?: string`.
- Gateway schema (`CronCommonOptionalFields`, `CronJobSchema`) exposes
  the new fields for `cron.add`, `cron.update`, and list/status responses.
- `timer.ts` enqueues the chained job via `CommandLane.Cron` after each
  scheduled or manual run; guards against self-loops; uses a lazy
  `import('./ops.js')` to avoid a circular dependency.

**Web UI (`ui/`)**
- `CronFormState` and `DEFAULT_CRON_FORM` carry `onSuccessJobId` /
  `onFailureJobId` string fields.
- `jobToForm` reads the fields from an existing job when editing.
- `addCronJob` sends `onSuccessJobId` / `onFailureJobId` (null to clear).
- Advanced form section gains a **Pipeline** subsection with two
  job-ID text inputs backed by a `<datalist>` of all known jobs for
  autocomplete.
- i18n keys in en / es / zh-CN.

## Test plan
- [x] All 59 cron unit tests pass (`pnpm test -- ui/src/ui/...`)
- [x] oxlint: 0 warnings / 0 errors on all changed files
- [x] oxfmt: all changed files formatted correctly

target.title: fix: resolve response prefix template variables in abort replies
target.body:
## Summary

Template variables are not populated when user issues early stop commands. 

- **Problem:** When a user sends a "stop" command (e.g. on WhatsApp), the abort reply displays unresolved template variables: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`
- **Why it matters:** The raw `{model}` and `{thinkingLevel}` placeholders leak into user-facing messages, looking broken and confusing.
- **What changed:** `createReplyPrefixContext()` now pre-seeds the `ResponsePrefixContext` with model, provider, and thinkingLevel resolved from config defaults, so early-exit paths like abort have valid values for template interpolation.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** No changes to abort logic, dispatch flow, template resolution engine, or `formatAbortReplyText()`. The `onModelSelected()` callback still overwrites the defaults at runtime during normal replies.

## Change Type (select all)

- [X] Bug fix

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [X] Gateway / orchestration
- [X] Integrations

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #45314

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Abort replies now display the resolved model name and thinking level from config (e.g. `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`) instead of literal `{model} - {thinkingLevel}` placeholders.
- Applies to all channels using a `responsePrefix` template with `{model}` or `{thinkingLevel}` variables.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / pnpm
- Model/provider: openrouter/moonshotai/kimi-k2.5
- Integration/channel: WhatsApp (reproduces on any channel with `responsePrefix` template)
- Relevant config:
  ```json
  {
    "messages": {
      "responsePrefix": "{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: "
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure a `responsePrefix` with `{model}` and/or `{thinkingLevel}` template variables
2. Start a conversation so the agent is processing
3. Send "stop" or `/stop`

### Expected

- Reply: `kimi-k2.5 - off 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

### Actual

- Reply: `{model} - {thinkingLevel} 🦞: ⚙️ Agent was aborted.`

## Evidence
Before
![before](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/73faefe5-65c3-488d-a429-94edf51ca959)
After
![after](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0e70f899-9353-4679-a63a-4272a8d7d197)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: `/stop` command triggers abort reply with resolved prefix; normal replies still resolve correctly after model selection overwrites defaults
- Edge cases checked: No model configured (falls back to hardcoded defaults); `onModelSelected` correctly overwrites pre-seeded values at runtime

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the single commit
- Files/config to restore: `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts`
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Incorrect model name or thinking level in reply prefix (would indicate config resolution mismatch)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: `resolveDefaultModelForAgent()` or `resolveThinkingDefault()` could add overhead to context creation.
  - Mitigation: Both are lightweight synchronous config lookups with no I/O; already used elsewhere in the hot path.

---

## Files Changed

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.ts` | Pre-seed `ResponsePrefixContext` with default model/provider/thinkingLevel from config |
| `src/channels/reply-prefix.test.ts` | New test: verifies pre-seeded context, `onModelSelected` override, and fallback defaults |


---

## Notes

- This PR is AI-assisted (fully tested: `pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test` — 919 test files, 2642 tests passing).
- Add before/after screenshots showing the abort reply with unresolved vs resolved template variables.
- The root cause is that the abort fast-path in `dispatch-from-config.ts` exits before the agent run triggers `onModelSelected()`, so the prefix context never got model/thinkingLevel populated. The fix is entirely in context initialization — no changes to abort or dispatch logic.

target.title: feat(queue): persist followup queues across gateway restarts
target.body:
## Problem

When the gateway process restarts (npm update, crash, systemd restart), any
messages that arrived while an agent turn was in progress are silently lost.
The followup queue lives only in process memory — there is no recovery path.

This is particularly noticeable on channels that serialize messages (e.g.
Telegram in queue mode): a message received during an active turn is queued
for delivery, then gone if the gateway restarts before that turn completes.

## Solution

Add a JSON persistence layer for the `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` global map.

### New file: `src/auto-reply/reply/queue/persist.ts`

- `persistFollowupQueues()` — serializes all non-empty queue entries to
  `<stateDir>/live-chat-followup-queues.json`. Respects `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`
  via the existing `resolveStateDir()` utility. State file is deleted when all
  queues drain to empty.
- `restoreFollowupQueues()` — reads the state file at module init and repopulates
  `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` before any enqueue or drain fires.
- `PersistedFollowupRun` — a `Pick<FollowupRun, ...>` type that explicitly
  excludes runtime-only, non-serializable fields: `abortSignal`,
  `currentTurnContext`, `deliveryCorrelations`, `queuedLifecycle`. These are
  transient and must not survive a restart.

### Mutation hooks

`persistFollowupQueues()` is called after every state-changing operation:

| Site | File |
|---|---|
| `enqueueFollowupRun` — after `items.push` | `enqueue.ts` |
| Drain `finally` block — both empty-queue delete and reschedule branches | `drain.ts` |
| `clearFollowupQueue` — after `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES.delete` | `state.ts` |
| `refreshQueuedFollowupSession` — after the run-rewrite loop | `state.ts` |

### Tests

`persist.test.ts` covers:
- State file written when queue has items
- State file deleted when all queues are empty
- Full round-trip: enqueue → persist → simulated restart → restore → verify prompt + routing fields
- Runtime-only `abortSignal` field not present after restore
- No-op when state file does not exist
- Malformed entries (non-array items, null key) are skipped

## Notes

- The `draining: false` invariant is enforced on restore — any in-flight drain
  that was interrupted is restarted by the normal drain scheduler on next enqueue.
- `summarySources` (contains `FollowupRun` objects, not serializable) is reset to
  `[]` on restore. `summaryLines` (plain strings) is persisted and restored.
- No schema migration needed for v1; unrecognized fields are ignored on restore.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior addressed: Queued followup messages (received while an agent turn is active) now survive gateway restarts. Before this patch a process restart discarded all pending items silently; after it they are written to `~/.openclaw/state/live-chat-followup-queues.json` and re-delivered once the drain callback is registered on startup.
- Real environment tested: Local dev, Node 22, Linux, openclaw source checkout (`jewseppi/openclaw` `feat/queue-followup-persistence`). Module loaded directly via `node --import tsx` — no test runner involved.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran `node --import tsx --input-type=module` against the live queue module to exercise the full persist → simulated-restart → restore cycle against a real temp directory.
- Evidence after fix: Terminal output from `node --import tsx` (live module, real filesystem I/O, no mocks):
  ```
  Step 1: persistFollowupQueues()
  State file: /tmp/openclaw-persist-proof-SdQ5bE/live-chat-followup-queues.json
  {
    "version": 1,
    "updatedAt": 1778974677687,
    "entries": [
      [
        "agent:main:dm:telegram-proof",
        {
          "items": [
            {
              "prompt": "did the gateway restart survive?",
              "enqueuedAt": 1778974677687,
              "originatingChannel": "telegram",
              "originatingTo": "99999",
              "run": {
                "agentId": "main",
                "agentDir": "/tmp/agent",
                "sessionId": "sess-proof",
                "sessionFile": "/tmp/sess.jsonl",
                "workspaceDir": "/tmp/ws",
                "provider": "anthropic",
                "model": "claude-sonnet-4-6",
                "timeoutMs": 30000,
                "blockReplyBreak": "message_end"
              }
            }
          ],
          "mode": "steer",
          "debounceMs": 500,
          "cap": 20,
          "dropPolicy": "summarize",
          "droppedCount": 0,
          "summaryLines": []
        }
      ]
    ]
  }

  Step 2: simulate restart — delete in-memory queue
    FOLLOWUP_QUEUES.get(KEY): undefined

  Step 3: restoreFollowupQueues()
    restored.items.length: 1
    restored.items[0].prompt: did the gateway restart survive?
    restored.items[0].originatingChannel: telegram
    restored.draining: false
    peekRestoredPendingDrainKeys().has(KEY): true

  Pass: queue survives a simulated process restart.
  ```
- Observed result after fix: 1 queued Telegram prompt survived the in-process restart simulation. `FOLLOWUP_QUEUES` was cleared to undefined, `restoreFollowupQueues()` re-populated it from the state file on disk, `draining` reset to `false`, and the key was registered in `restoredPendingDrainKeys` so drain scheduling fires when the callback is next registered. State file written and read by `node` against a real temp dir — no mocks, no vitest.
- What was not tested: Full live Telegram→gateway→kill→restart→re-delivery end-to-end (requires a real inbound message arriving during an active agent turn followed by a real process restart).

## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw doctor --fix` could miss stale non-Codex session runtime pins stored only in `agentRuntimeOverride`.
- Why it matters: old CLI runtime choices could keep later channel replies pinned to a runtime after the configured default moved back to PI or another provider.
- What changed: the generic Doctor session-route cleanup now treats plugin-owned `agentRuntimeOverride` values like `agentHarnessId`, and Anthropic/Google expose Doctor ownership for `claude-cli` / `google-gemini-cli`.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): runtime selection, model config shape, and explicit user model overrides are unchanged.

- Real environment tested: disposable local OpenClaw state home on the current beta.4 source checkout, no real credentials or network provider calls.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: seeded a real `sessions.json` with an `anthropic/claude-opus-4-7` session still carrying a stale `claude-cli` runtime override while the configured route runtime was `pi`, then ran the Doctor state-integrity repair path with repairs accepted.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
[sessions/store] pruned stale session entries
proof: disposable local OpenClaw home
configured route: anthropic/claude-opus-4-7 on pi
prompt: Clear stale Anthropic session routing state for 1 session?

same-provider claude runtime override: <removed>
same-provider model state: anthropic/claude-opus-4-7
current pi runtime override: <preserved>

target.title: fix: sync skills to workspace in rw mode
target.body:
Issue #48011

## Problem
When sandbox is configured with `workspaceAccess: "rw"`, non-workspace skills (managed and bundled skills) are inaccessible to the agent.

Error: `Path escapes sandbox root (~/.openclaw/workspaces/workspace-xxx): /usr/lib/node_modules/openclaw/extensions/feishu/skills/feishu-doc/SKILL.md`

## Root Cause
The `syncSkillsToWorkspace` call was wrapped in a condition that skipped it when `workspaceAccess === "rw"`:
```typescript
if (cfg.workspaceAccess !== "rw") {
  await syncSkillsToWorkspace({...});
}
```

Additionally, the `managedSkillsDir` and `bundledSkillsDir` parameters were not being passed, so system skills wouldn't be included even if sync did run.

## Fix
1. Remove the `workspaceAccess !== "rw"` check - always sync skills
2. Pass `managedSkillsDir` and `bundledSkillsDir` parameters so managed and bundled skills get copied into the sandbox workspace

This ensures agents in "rw" mode can access feishu-doc and other bundled/managed skills.

target.title: Dockerfile: simplify multi-stage build
target.body:
- Reduce from 5 stages to 2 (build + runtime)
- Remove retry loops for Bun and Corepack installs
- Hardcode bookworm-slim runtime (removes variant switching)
- Inline extension copy (removes ext-deps stage)
- Remove SHA256 digest pinning for simpler maintenance
- Preserve all original comments, variable names, and optional features

## Summary

- Problem: The Dockerfile uses 5 build stages, retry loops, variant switching via ARGs, and SHA256 digest pinning, making it difficult to read, maintain, and debug for contributors and self-hosters.
- Why it matters: A simpler Dockerfile lowers the barrier for contributions, speeds up local iteration, and reduces the surface area for build failures caused by stage interdependencies.
- What changed: Consolidated 5 stages into 2 (build + runtime), removed retry loops for Bun/Corepack installs, hardcoded bookworm-slim as the runtime base, replaced the ext-deps stage with a direct COPY, and removed SHA256 digest pinning in favor of standard image tags.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): All variable names (`OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES`, `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_BROWSER`, `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_DOCKER_CLI`, `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_GPG_FINGERPRINT`), optional feature blocks (browser, Docker CLI, extra apt packages), security hardening (non-root user, healthcheck), build steps (`pnpm install`, a2ui bundle, build:docker, ui:build, prune), and the runtime CMD are unchanged.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- The `OPENCLAW_VARIANT` build arg is removed; the runtime image is always based on `node:24-bookworm-slim`. Users who explicitly built with `OPENCLAW_VARIANT=default` (full bookworm) will need to adjust or use the slim image.
- The `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS` build arg and selective extension filtering are removed; all extensions in the `extensions/` directory are now copied unconditionally.
- Image tags are no longer pinned to SHA256 digests; builds will pull the latest `node:24-bookworm` and `node:24-bookworm-slim` tags.
- Bun and Corepack installs no longer retry on transient failures; builds on flaky networks may need manual retries.
- APT layer caching uses `rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*` instead of named `--mount=type=cache` mounts.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No` - same upstream sources (bun.sh, npm registry, apt repos, Docker GPG key)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No`

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux (Arch Linux Testing)
- Runtime/container: Docker 29.3.0 with BuildKit/buildx v0.32.1
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Default build with no optional build args

### Steps

1. `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build -t openclaw-simplified .`
2. `docker run --rm openclaw-simplified node -e "console.log('ok')"`
3. `docker run --rm openclaw-simplified node openclaw.mjs gateway --help`

### Expected

- Image builds successfully in 2 stages
- Container starts and runs Node.js commands
- Gateway help output is displayed

### Actual

- Image built successfully in 2 stages (`build` 15 steps + `stage-1` 16 steps) in ~192s
  - Final image: `openclaw-simplified:latest` - 4.08GB disk / 1.02GB content (sha256:a09af4b5f88c)
- Container starts and runs Node.js: `docker run --rm openclaw-simplified node -e "console.log('ok')"` → `ok`
- Gateway help output displayed: `docker run --rm openclaw-simplified node openclaw.mjs gateway --help` → full help text with all subcommands rendered correctly
- OpenClaw version: `2026.3.14 (unknown)`
- Runtime user: `node` (non-root, uid 1000)

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets [openclaw-build.txt](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/26009537/openclaw-build.txt)

Full build + verification log attached: [openclaw-build.log](cci:7://file:///home/szoltysek/openclaw-build.log:0:0-0:0) (228 lines)
Contains: BuildKit build output (39 steps, all succeeded), node smoke test (`ok`), `gateway --help` full output, and `docker images` size info.

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Built the image locally with `DOCKER_BUILDKIT=1 docker build -t openclaw-simplified .`; ran `node -e "console.log('ok')"` in the container; ran `node openclaw.mjs gateway --help` and confirmed full help output; verified image size with `docker images`.
- Edge cases checked: Optional build args (`OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES`, `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_BROWSER`, `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_DOCKER_CLI`) are still present and conditionally executed (confirmed by build log showing `if [ -n "" ]` no-op steps); A2UI bundle fallback stub logic is preserved.
- What you did **not** verify: Cross-architecture builds (ARM/QEMU); builds with optional args enabled (`OPENCLAW_INSTALL_BROWSER=1`, `OPENCLAW_INSTALL_DOCKER_CLI=1`); performance comparison against the original 5-stage Dockerfile.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `No` - the `OPENCLAW_VARIANT` and `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS` build args are removed.
- Config/env changes? `Yes` - users relying on `OPENCLAW_VARIANT=default` (full bookworm) or selective `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS` filtering need to adjust.
- Migration needed? `Yes`
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:
  1. If you used `--build-arg OPENCLAW_VARIANT=default`, remove that arg. The runtime is now always bookworm-slim. If you need packages only in full bookworm, add them via `--build-arg OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES="..."`.
  2. If you used `--build-arg OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS="ext1 ext2"`, remove that arg. All extensions are now copied. To exclude extensions, remove them from the `extensions/` directory before building.
  3. If you pinned builds to specific SHA256 digests, note that builds now track the `node:24-bookworm` / `node:24-bookworm-slim` rolling tags.

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert the single `Dockerfile` to the previous commit: `git revert <commit-sha>`
- Files/config to restore: `Dockerfile` (single file change)
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
  - `curl: (22) The requested URL returned error: 5xx` during Bun install (no retry loop - re-run the build)
  - Missing extensions at runtime if the `extensions/` directory was not fully populated at build time
  - Larger image size if unneeded extensions are included (previously filtered by OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS)

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Bun or Corepack install fails on transient network errors without retry loops.
  - Mitigation: Docker/Buildx layer caching means re-running the build retries only the failed layer. CI systems typically have their own retry mechanisms. The retry loops can be re-added if this proves to be a recurring issue in CI.

- Risk: Removing SHA256 digest pinning means builds are no longer fully reproducible - the `node:24-bookworm` tag can move.
  - Mitigation: For production/release builds, users can pin digests in their own `docker-compose.yml` or CI pipeline. The simplification benefits outweigh the reproducibility trade-off for the majority of users.

- Risk: Removing `OPENCLAW_EXTENSIONS` filtering may increase image size if many extensions exist.
  - Mitigation: Extensions are typically small (package.json + JS files). The ext-deps stage only saved space for users with many unused extensions. Users needing minimal images can prune `extensions/` before building.

- Risk: Removing the full bookworm variant may break users who depend on packages only available in the non-slim image.
  - Mitigation: The `OPENCLAW_DOCKER_APT_PACKAGES` build arg remains available to install any additional packages. The slim image + explicit apt installs is the more common Docker pattern.

target.title: feat(plugin-sdk): add reply payload sending hook
target.body:
## Summary
- add a new \`reply_payload_sending\` plugin hook that can mutate or cancel normalized \`ReplyPayload\` objects before delivery
- wire the hook into auto-reply dispatch so plugins can modify presentation, delivery metadata, and text at the normalized payload stage
- export the new hook types through the plugin SDK, document the new hook, and add coverage for chaining, cancellation, and dispatch-time mutation

## Why
Today plugin authors can:
- rewrite plain text with \`message_sending\`
- intercept or replace dispatches with \`before_dispatch\`
- take over reply delivery with \`reply_dispatch\`

But there is no hook that exposes the actual normalized outbound \`ReplyPayload\` before channel delivery. That makes clean plugin-only features like attaching Telegram inline buttons to normal assistant replies hard or impossible without core patches.

This change adds a focused seam for that use case while reusing the existing before-deliver phase in the reply dispatcher.

## What changed
- added \`reply_payload_sending\` to \`PluginHookName\` and \`PLUGIN_HOOK_NAMES\`
- added new hook event/context/result types:
  - \`PluginHookReplyPayloadSendingEvent\`
  - \`PluginHookReplyPayloadSendingContext\`
  - \`PluginHookReplyPayloadSendingResult\`
- added \`runReplyPayloadSending()\` to the hook runner
- wired the new hook into \`dispatchInboundMessageWithDispatcher()\` and \`dispatchInboundMessageWithBufferedDispatcher()\` via dispatcher \`beforeDeliver\`
- preserved sequential mutation semantics so each lower-priority handler sees the latest payload
- allowed cancellation by returning \`{ cancel: true }\`
- exported the new hook types from \`src/plugin-sdk/core.ts\`
- documented the new hook in \`docs/plugins/hooks.md\`
- added tests for:
  - hook-runner payload chaining
  - cancellation behavior
  - error isolation
  - dispatch-time payload mutation with run/session/channel context

## Verification
Targeted local verification completed:
- \`corepack pnpm tsgo:core:test\`
- \`node scripts/test-projects.mjs --maxWorkers=1 src/plugins/wired-hooks-reply-payload-sending.test.ts\`
- \`node scripts/test-projects.mjs --maxWorkers=1 src/auto-reply/dispatch.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/before-deliver.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.reply-dispatch.test.ts\`
- \`corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --check docs/plugins/hooks.md src/auto-reply/dispatch.test.ts src/auto-reply/dispatch.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.shared.test-harness.ts src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-from-config.test.ts src/plugin-sdk/core.ts src/plugins/hook-types.ts src/plugins/hooks.ts src/plugins/wired-hooks-reply-payload-sending.test.ts\`

Repo-wide \`pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test\` was not run end-to-end on this machine for this PR; validation here was focused on the touched hook, dispatch, docs, and real Telegram behavior path.

## Live validation
- validated the real local usage path with the installed \`telegram-final-buttons\` plugin, which uses \`reply_payload_sending\` to attach inline buttons to Telegram final replies
- confirmed via gateway logs that the plugin attached buttons and Telegram delivered the reply successfully
- captured local screenshot proof of the rendered Telegram inline buttons in Telegram Desktop
- visible buttons in the screenshot:
  - \`Proceed\`
  - \`Status\`
  - \`Recommendation\`

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed**: Plugin authors need a way to mutate normalized outbound reply payloads before channel delivery so plugin-only features such as Telegram inline buttons can be added without core channel-specific patches.

**Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw gateway running against a real Telegram bot and Telegram Desktop on the host machine, using the installed local \`telegram-final-buttons\` plugin.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
1. Start the local OpenClaw gateway with the patched \`reply_payload_sending\` hook available.
2. Keep the local \`telegram-final-buttons\` plugin enabled so it uses \`reply_payload_sending\` to append inline buttons to final Telegram replies.
3. Send messages through the real Telegram bot into the test thread.
4. Observe the resulting bot reply in Telegram Desktop.

**Evidence after fix**: Gateway logs showed the live \`telegram-final-buttons\` plugin attaching buttons to a Telegram final reply and Telegram delivering the reply successfully. A local Telegram Desktop screenshot was captured showing the rendered inline buttons.

**Observed result after fix**: The Telegram bot reply rendered native inline buttons in the real client, including \`Proceed\`, \`Status\`, and \`Recommendation\`.

**What was not tested**: Fresh linked-install validation from the temporary fork-side demo package was not the final proof path; the real behavior proof used the already-installed local plugin path instead.

## AI assistance
This PR was AI-assisted. I used Codex to help implement, test, validate, and iterate on the change, and I reviewed the resulting code and behavior locally before submission.

## Notes
- this intentionally hooks the normalized payload stage instead of only raw text so plugins can safely add presentation blocks, media, delivery hints, and similar metadata
- this follows the same general precedence model as other modifying hooks: handlers run sequentially, and cancellation is terminal
- the screenshot proof came from the already-installed local plugin path, not from a fresh linked-install of the temporary demo package in the fork

target.title: fix(gateway): preserve raw external session aliases
target.body:
Closes #63005.

## Behavior addressed

External raw session keys such as `conversation:pair:...` should survive gateway session-key canonicalization. A nested `sessions_send` or agent-scoped lookup should resolve to the same stored session instead of splitting history across raw and `agent:<id>:` aliases.

## Root cause

`migrateAndPruneGatewaySessionStoreKey` canonicalized raw external keys before ensuring the raw-key store entry was copied to the canonical agent key. The first fix preserved that raw alias, but the mutation lifecycle still replaced or deleted only the canonical `primaryKey`. That left the raw alias stale after `sessions.patch`, `sessions.delete`, `sessions.reset`, agent writeback, and node-event store touches.

## Fix

The migration helper now returns explicit `preservedAliasKeys`. Shared store helpers synchronize those aliases on writes and remove them on delete. The mutation paths that replace session entries now use the shared write helper, and `sessions.delete` removes both canonical and preserved raw aliases. Preserved raw aliases stay internal-only for lookup continuity: `sessions.list` filtering/search/count collapses a raw+canonical alias pair into one visible canonical session row.

Regression coverage now includes:

- raw external migrate/write/delete helper behavior in `src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- `sessions.patch` and `sessions.delete` raw-alias lifecycle coverage in `src/gateway/server.sessions.store-rpc.test.ts`
- `sessions.reset` raw-alias lifecycle coverage in `src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-cleanup.test.ts`
- raw+canonical alias collapse coverage for list/search/count in `src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts`
- resolver visibility contract coverage in `src/gateway/sessions-resolve.test.ts`, covering the stale mock that failed CI on an earlier head
- node-event runtime mock coverage for the new shared write helper in `src/gateway/server-node-events.test.ts`

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior or issue addressed:** Raw external session aliases are preserved during canonicalization, stay synchronized when the canonical entry is replaced or deleted, and do not duplicate visible list/search/count rows.

**Real environment tested:** PR head `e721539cff63fc1350c185113fe329ab0eb7848b`, rebased onto `upstream/main` `3e6f7494af16f47e2d9c257f3c16854a340bd147`; source checkout with Node/tsx for the targeted proof command.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
env OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=<minimal-temp-config> OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=<empty-temp-state-dir> node --import tsx -e 'const { migrateAndPruneGatewaySessionStoreKey, writeGatewaySessionStoreEntry, deleteGatewaySessionStoreEntry, listSessionsFromStore } = await import("./src/gateway/session-utils.ts"); const cfg = { session: { mainKey: "main" }, agents: { list: [{ id: "main", default: true }], defaults: { model: { primary: "openai/gpt-5.4" } } } }; const rawKey = "conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123"; const canonicalKey = `agent:main:${rawKey}`; const store = { [rawKey]: { sessionId: "sess-raw", updatedAt: 1, subject: "Alias Needle" } }; const migrated = migrateAndPruneGatewaySessionStoreKey({ cfg, key: rawKey, store }); writeGatewaySessionStoreEntry({ store, primaryKey: migrated.primaryKey, aliasKeys: migrated.preservedAliasKeys, entry: { sessionId: "sess-write", updatedAt: 2, sendPolicy: "deny", subject: "Alias Needle" } }); const listed = listSessionsFromStore({ cfg, storePath: "", store, opts: {} }); const searched = listSessionsFromStore({ cfg, storePath: "", store, opts: { search: "alias needle" } }); const afterWrite = { canonicalSessionId: store[canonicalKey]?.sessionId ?? null, rawSessionId: store[rawKey]?.sessionId ?? null, rawSendPolicy: store[rawKey]?.sendPolicy ?? null, sameEntry: store[canonicalKey] === store[rawKey] }; const visible = { listKeys: listed.sessions.map((session) => session.key), listCount: listed.count, listTotalCount: listed.totalCount, searchKeys: searched.sessions.map((session) => session.key), searchCount: searched.count, searchTotalCount: searched.totalCount }; const deleted = deleteGatewaySessionStoreEntry({ store, primaryKey: migrated.primaryKey, aliasKeys: migrated.preservedAliasKeys }); console.log(JSON.stringify({ primaryKey: migrated.primaryKey, preservedAliasKeys: migrated.preservedAliasKeys, afterWrite, visible, deleted, afterDelete: { canonicalSessionId: store[canonicalKey]?.sessionId ?? null, rawSessionId: store[rawKey]?.sessionId ?? null } }, null, 2));'
```

**Evidence after fix:** Terminal output from the direct after-fix command:

```json
{
  "primaryKey": "agent:main:conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123",
  "preservedAliasKeys": [
    "conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123"
  ],
  "afterWrite": {
    "canonicalSessionId": "sess-write",
    "rawSessionId": "sess-write",
    "rawSendPolicy": "deny",
    "sameEntry": true
  },
  "visible": {
    "listKeys": [
      "agent:main:conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123"
    ],
    "listCount": 1,
    "listTotalCount": 1,
    "searchKeys": [
      "agent:main:conversation:pair:user_a::user_b:space:space_123"
    ],
    "searchCount": 1,
    "searchTotalCount": 1
  },
  "deleted": true,
  "afterDelete": {
    "canonicalSessionId": null,
    "rawSessionId": null
  }
}
```

**Observed result after fix:** The raw alias and canonical key share the same written entry after replacement, including the patched `sendPolicy`; list and search both return one visible canonical row with `totalCount: 1`; delete removes both keys. The targeted server tests also cover `sessions.patch`, `sessions.delete`, and `sessions.reset` against the real gateway handlers.

**What was not tested:** I did not run a full live chat-channel round trip for this backend store migration. There is no visual UI surface for this fix, so screenshots are not applicable.

Additional checks after the latest rebase:

- `pnpm docs:list` ran before editing.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.store-rpc.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-cleanup.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts src/gateway/server-node-events.test.ts src/gateway/sessions-resolve.test.ts` passed: `Test Files 11 passed (11)`, `Tests 546 passed (546)`.
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/gateway/sessions-resolve.ts` passed: `Found 0 warnings and 0 errors`.
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` passed.
- `./node_modules/.bin/oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/gateway/session-utils.ts src/gateway/session-utils.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts src/gateway/session-reset-service.ts src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts src/gateway/server-node-events.ts src/gateway/server-node-events.runtime.ts src/gateway/sessions-resolve.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.store-rpc.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-cleanup.test.ts src/gateway/server-node-events.test.ts src/gateway/sessions-resolve.test.ts` passed.
- Broad Testbox changed gate was not run because the `blacksmith` CLI is not installed in this environment; `pnpm changed:lanes --json` expanded beyond this PR, so I did not run the broad local gate.

commands.author: hclsys

target.title: fix(gateway): emit session_start hook on agent.send auto-rotation (#83507)
target.body:
Fixes #83507.

`agent.send`'s auto-rotation branch (daily-reset boundary OR client-supplied new sessionId) writes a new sessionId directly via `updateSessionStore` and emits `sessions.changed reason="create"` — but **bypasses** `emitGatewaySessionStartPluginHook`. Every other session-creation path emits the hook:

- `sessions.reset` → `src/gateway/session-reset-service.ts:836`
- `sessions.create` (with `emitCommandHooks=true`) → `src/gateway/server-methods/sessions.ts:1326`
- `/new` and `/reset` slash commands in agent input → same canonical path

So plugins listening on `session_start` silently missed the **most common** new-session trigger: a user simply resuming a chat after the 4am daily boundary. The reporter's workaround was a dual-source state machine subscribing to `sessions.subscribe` and treating `reason="create"` as a synonymous trigger.

## Changes

- `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts`: import `emitGatewaySessionStartPluginHook` from `../session-reset-service.js` (already had `performGatewaySessionReset` from the same module); call it immediately after the existing `emitSessionsChanged({reason:"create"})` inside the `if (requestedSessionKey && resolvedSessionKey && isNewSession)` branch. Argument shape matches the canonical `sessions.reset` site (`cfg`, `sessionKey`, `sessionId`, `storePath`, `sessionFile`, `agentId`).
- The hook function already early-returns when `sessionId` is missing (`session-reset-service.ts:199-201`), so the call is safe even if `resolvedSessionId` is somehow undefined at this point.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Plugins registered for `session_start` were not invoked when `agent.send` rotated a `sessionKey`'s `sessionId` across the daily boundary or via a client-supplied new id. The fix makes the hook fire in lockstep with the existing `sessions.changed reason="create"` event for that branch.

- **Real environment tested:** Local Node 22.x. Probe at `/tmp/probe_83507.mjs` parses the patched `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts` and the unchanged `src/gateway/session-reset-service.ts` and verifies (a) the new import, (b) the hook call sits inside the existing `isNewSession` branch, (c) the argument shape matches the canonical sessions.reset call site, (d) the hook's existing `sessionId` guard is preserved.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node /tmp/probe_83507.mjs`

- **Evidence after fix:**

```
PASS: emitGatewaySessionStartPluginHook imported
PASS: emitGatewaySessionStartPluginHook called inside the isNewSession branch
PASS: new hook call passes cfg/sessionKey/sessionId/storePath/sessionFile/agentId
PASS: session-reset-service.ts has 2 references (definition + canonical caller)
PASS: hook still early-returns when sessionId missing (safety preserved)

ALL CASES PASS
```

- **Observed result after fix:** Source-level the new emit site is structurally equivalent to the existing `sessions.reset` emit site — same hook function, same argument shape, gated by the same `isNewSession` boolean that gates the paired `emitSessionsChanged({reason:"create"})` call. Plugins listening on `session_start` will now receive the rotation event.

- **What was not tested:** End-to-end run with a live plugin subscribing to `session_start`, sending an `agent.send` across the daily reset, and observing the hook fire. No live gateway available in this environment. The existing giant `agent.test.ts` integration suite does not exercise the `session_start` plugin hook for this branch, and adding meaningful unit coverage would require substantial mock scaffolding (hook runner + plugin loader + active-sessions tracker mocks); the source-level probe verifies the structural invariant directly.

## Audit (per CLAUDE rules — all 5 steps)

- **Existing-helper check:** Reuses `emitGatewaySessionStartPluginHook` from `session-reset-service.js` (the canonical hook emitter the reset path already uses). No new function. PASS
- **Shared-helper caller check:** The helper is currently called from `session-reset-service.ts:836` (the reset path) and tested in `drain-active-sessions-for-shutdown.test.ts`. Adding a new caller doesn't mutate the helper's contract — it's designed to be called from any session-creation path. PASS
- **Broader-fix rival scan:** No open PRs touching `agent.ts` auto-rotation / `emitGatewaySessionStartPluginHook` / `session_start` hook missing-on-rotation. PASS
- **Recent-merge audit:** `git log --oneline -10 -- src/gateway/server-methods/agent.ts` shows `91f45d9c8a fix(gateway): dedupe exec followup continuations (#82717)` — unrelated. No recent merges that would conflict. PASS
- **Prototype-pollution scan:** N/A — no external-input key copying.

target.title: Doctor: convert read-only health checks
target.body:
## Summary

This is the first focused follow-up to #80055. It keeps the conversion deliberately low-risk by moving read-only doctor checks into health-check `detect()` findings while preserving legacy `doctor --fix` behavior until each repair area has a real modern `repair()` owner.

This PR adds:
- a checked conversion plan that maps existing doctor contributions to their target health checks;
- health-check target metadata on doctor contributions;
- read-only findings for security, browser readiness, workspace suggestions, legacy WhatsApp crontab warnings, and gateway platform notes. Session snapshots stay on the legacy doctor run path in this slice until they have a structured detector.

## Behavior addressed

Doctor follow-up conversions are easier to review when each area moves from `run(ctx)` to `detect()` independently. This PR converts read-only checks first, so `doctor --lint` can report these findings through structured health output without changing repair behavior.

## Migration rule

This PR treats the first slice as a reporting conversion, not a repair takeover.

`doctor --lint` uses the new `detect()` findings for converted checks. `doctor --fix` keeps the existing legacy `run()` / `maybeRepair*()` behavior unless a specific check has a complete modern `repair()` implementation. Placeholder health checks are inventory targets only; they do not suppress legacy notes or repairs yet. Checks that do not yet have a structured detector are deliberately left off the lint registry to avoid false-clean results.

That means later area-specific PRs can safely move one check at a time: first add structured detection, then add scoped repair, then remove or skip the legacy path only when the modern path fully owns the behavior.

## Staging note

This PR is stacked on current `main` after #80055 landed. The full conversion branch is intentionally not included here; future follow-ups should be opened area by area after this first read-only slice.

## Real behavior proof

![PR 83198 before/after proof](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83198-fresh/pr-83198/pr-83198-doctor-convert-read-only-health-checks-before-after-proof.png)

Fresh deterministic proof artifacts:
- Summary: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83198-fresh/pr-83198/summary.txt
- Before/source proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83198-fresh/pr-83198/before-source-proof.log
- After/PR proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83198-fresh/pr-83198/after-pr-83198.log

Behavior addressed: This PR converts read-only doctor checks to structured health `detect()` findings so `doctor --lint` can report them without repair-mode execution.

Real environment tested: WSL Ubuntu source checkout, branch `doctor-detection-conversion-plan`, based on `upstream/main` after #80055.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
tmp=$(mktemp -d)
OPENCLAW_HOME="$tmp/.openclaw" pnpm openclaw doctor --lint --only core/doctor/workspace-suggestions
```

Evidence after fix: The real OpenClaw CLI ran one converted read-only doctor check and emitted a structured lint finding without entering repair mode.

```text
$ node scripts/run-node.mjs doctor --lint --only core/doctor/workspace-suggestions
{"ok":false,"checksRun":1,"checksSkipped":44,"findings":[{"checkId":"core/doctor/workspace-suggestions","severity":"info","message":"Memory system not found in workspace.","fixHint":"Paste this into your agent:\n\nInstall the memory system by applying:\nhttps://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/9ffea23f31ca1df5183b25668f8f814bee0fb34e\nhttps://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/commit/7d1fee70e76f2f634f1b41fca927ee663914183a"}]}
[ELIFECYCLE] Command failed with exit code 1.
```

Observed result after fix: `doctor --lint` executed the selected converted check in read-only mode, reported `core/doctor/workspace-suggestions` as a structured finding, skipped the other 44 checks because of `--only`, and returned the expected lint-failure exit when a finding was present.

Supplemental verification:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/flows/doctor-health-conversion-plan.test.ts src/flows/doctor-core-checks.test.ts src/commands/doctor-lint.test.ts src/commands/doctor-cron.test.ts src/commands/doctor-platform-notes.launchctl-env-overrides.test.ts src/flows/doctor-repair-flow.test.ts
node_modules/.bin/tsgo -p tsconfig.core.json --noEmit --incremental false --pretty false
git diff --check
```

Supplemental result: Focused Vitest proof passed: 6 files, 43 tests. Core typecheck passed. `git diff --check` passed with no whitespace/conflict-marker findings.

What was not tested: Full repository CI and broad Crabbox/Testbox validation were not run locally for this draft; this branch is intended as a narrow follow-up behind #80055.

target.title: fix(config): allow bundled provider timeout overlays
target.body:
Fixes #83201.

## Summary

- Allow bundled model provider config entries to act as overlays, so `models.providers.openai.timeoutSeconds` is valid without redeclaring `baseUrl` and `models`.
- Preserve strict custom-provider validation: unknown provider IDs still require both `baseUrl` and `models`.
- Guard configured-provider fallback against overlay entries without `models`, and prove the timeout overlay is applied during model resolution.

## Real behavior proof

![PR 83267 before/after proof](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83267-fresh/pr-83267/pr-83267-fix-config-allow-bundled-provider-timeout-overla-before-after-proof.png)

Fresh deterministic proof artifacts:
- Summary: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83267-fresh/pr-83267/summary.txt
- Before/source proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83267-fresh/pr-83267/before-source-proof.log
- After/PR proof log: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-83267-fresh/pr-83267/after-pr-83267.log

**Behavior or issue addressed:** A documented config such as `models.providers.openai.timeoutSeconds = 600` was rejected at startup because the validator required `models.providers.openai.baseUrl` and `models.providers.openai.models`, even though the entry is only tuning a bundled provider.

**Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout on Ubuntu 24.04 under WSL, running production config validation and model resolution against real config objects.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a source-level before/after probe against `origin/main` and this PR head, then ran focused validation and changed-file checks:

```text
node --import tsx <provider-timeout-overlay-proof>
CI=1 node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/config/config-misc.test.ts src/config/schema.test.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.test.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.google-antigravity.test.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.implicit.discovery-scope.test.ts src/agents/models-config.providers.normalize-keys.test.ts
pnpm check:changed
```

**Evidence after fix:** The before/after probe shows the bundled `openai` overlay now validates, while an unknown custom provider with only `timeoutSeconds` still fails:

```text
before_status=10
{
  "overlay": {
    "ok": false,
    "issues": [
      "models.providers.openai.baseUrl: Invalid input: expected string, received undefined",
      "models.providers.openai.models: Invalid input: expected array, received undefined"
    ]
  },
  "custom": {
    "ok": false,
    "issues": [
      "models.providers.custom.baseUrl: Invalid input: expected string, received undefined",
      "models.providers.custom.models: Invalid input: expected array, received undefined"
    ]
  }
}
---
after_status=0
{
  "overlay": {
    "ok": true,
    "issues": []
  },
  "custom": {
    "ok": false,
    "issues": [
      "models.providers.custom.baseUrl: custom model providers must declare baseUrl; provider overlays without baseUrl are only supported for bundled providers",
      "models.providers.custom.models: custom model providers must declare models; provider overlays without models are only supported for bundled providers"
    ]
  }
}
```

Focused tests passed:

```text
src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts: 22 tests passed
src/config/schema.test.ts: 39 tests passed
src/config/config-misc.test.ts: 81 tests passed
src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.test.ts: 84 tests passed in both agents-core and agents-pi-embedded shards
pnpm check:changed: passed
```

**Observed result after fix:** `models.providers.openai.timeoutSeconds` can be used as a standalone bundled provider overlay and the resolver applies it as `requestTimeoutMs=600000`; a custom `models.providers.custom.timeoutSeconds` entry still fails validation until it declares `baseUrl` and `models`.

**What was not tested:** No live OpenAI request was sent; this validates startup config behavior and the model-resolution metadata path without contacting provider APIs.
